structural_observation: Quantum computing's primary social risks are asymmetric rather than distributed. Smartphones harmed everyone who used them. AI harms scale across populations. Quantum computing's deepest risks come from the fact that very few entities will have it and most will not — and the gap between those two groups is where the danger lives.

issues.name: Cryptographic collapse — the Q-Day problem
issues.description: A sufficiently powerful fault-tolerant quantum computer breaks RSA, ECC, and most public-key cryptography securing banking, communications, government systems, and critical infrastructure. Mathematically certain once the hardware threshold is crossed.

reformers.name: NIST Post-Quantum Cryptography Project
reformers.description: The National Institute of Standards and Technology launched its Post-Quantum Cryptography Standardization process in 2016, evaluating 82 initial submissions and narrowing to final standards. In August 2024, NIST published FIPS 203 (ML-KEM, based on CRYSTALS-Kyber), FIPS 204 (ML-DSA, based on CRYSTALS-Dilithium), and FIPS 205 (SLH-DSA, based on SPHINCS+). This is the single most consequential effort to prepare global infrastructure for Q-Day, as NIST standards are adopted worldwide.

reformers.name: Dustin Moody
reformers.description: Mathematician at NIST who has led the Post-Quantum Cryptography Standardization project since its inception. Moody has coordinated the multi-year evaluation process involving hundreds of researchers globally, managing the difficult balance between urgency and rigor in selecting algorithms that must protect data for decades.

reformers.name: Open Quantum Safe (OQS) Project
reformers.description: An open-source initiative founded at the University of Waterloo that provides prototype implementations of post-quantum cryptographic algorithms. OQS maintains liboqs, a C library of quantum-resistant algorithms, and integration into OpenSSL, enabling developers and organizations to begin testing and migrating to post-quantum cryptography before the threat materializes. Critical for making the transition accessible beyond well-resourced entities.

reformers.name: Michele Mosca
reformers.description: Co-founder of the Institute for Quantum Computing at the University of Waterloo and a leading voice on quantum risk timelines. Mosca developed the widely-cited 'Mosca theorem' for quantum risk assessment — if the time to migrate systems plus the shelf life of data exceeds the time to a cryptographically relevant quantum computer, you are already too late. His framework has driven urgency in government and corporate planning.

reformers.name: Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA)
reformers.description: CISA has issued guidance and roadmaps for federal agencies and critical infrastructure operators to begin post-quantum migration planning. Their 2022 directive on preparing for post-quantum cryptography and ongoing collaboration with NIST represents the operational arm of the US government's Q-Day preparation, translating standards into actionable transition plans for thousands of organizations.

reformers.name: Signal Foundation
reformers.description: Signal became one of the first major messaging platforms to deploy post-quantum key agreement (PQXDH protocol) in September 2023, using a hybrid approach combining X25519 with CRYSTALS-Kyber. By deploying PQC in a widely-used consumer product, Signal demonstrated that migration is feasible and set a standard that pressures other messaging platforms to follow.

legislation.name: Quantum Computing Cybersecurity Preparedness Act
legislation.description: Signed into law in December 2022, this act requires federal agencies to inventory their cryptographic systems, assess quantum vulnerability, and develop migration plans to post-quantum cryptography standards as they become available from NIST. Establishes reporting requirements to OMB and sets timelines for transition planning.

legislation.name: National Security Memorandum NSM-10 on Quantum Computing
legislation.description: Issued by President Biden in May 2022, NSM-10 directed federal agencies to begin migration to quantum-resistant cryptography and established the timeline and process for transition. It mandated inventory of cryptographic systems and set a goal of full migration by 2035, while acknowledging that some national security systems require faster timelines.

legislation.name: No federal mandate for private sector PQC migration
legislation.description: While federal agencies have NSM-10 and the Quantum Computing Cybersecurity Preparedness Act, there is no equivalent legal requirement for private sector companies — including critical infrastructure operators, hospitals, utilities, and financial institutions — to migrate to post-quantum cryptography. This structural gap means the most vulnerable systems may be the last to transition.

legislation.name: National Quantum Initiative Act
legislation.description: Passed in 2018, this act established a coordinated federal program to accelerate quantum information science research and development. While primarily a funding and coordination mechanism ($1.2 billion authorized), it created the National Quantum Coordination Office and advisory committees. However, it focuses more on quantum advancement than on mitigating quantum risks, potentially accelerating the timeline to Q-Day without commensurate defensive preparation.

legislation.name: CNSA 2.0 (Commercial National Security Algorithm Suite 2.0)
legislation.description: Issued by the NSA in September 2022, CNSA 2.0 provides the timeline and algorithm requirements for national security systems to transition to quantum-resistant cryptography. It mandates specific algorithms and sets deadlines — software and firmware signing by 2025, web browsers and servers by 2025, traditional networking equipment by 2026, operating systems by 2027, and full transition of remaining systems by 2033.

legislation.name: Absence of quantum-specific liability frameworks
legislation.description: No US law establishes liability for organizations that fail to migrate to post-quantum cryptography and subsequently suffer breaches due to quantum-enabled attacks. Without clear liability, organizations lack legal incentive to undertake costly migration, and affected individuals have no clear recourse when their data is compromised through quantum decryption of legacy-encrypted material.

structurally_incentivized.name: Legacy enterprise software vendors
structurally_incentivized.description: Companies selling enterprise systems built on classical cryptographic assumptions face massive costs to re-engineer their products. Many have financial incentive to downplay the urgency of Q-Day to delay expensive re-engineering and avoid customer migration to competitors who offer PQC-ready solutions.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Revenue from existing installed base depends on minimizing perceived urgency of migration; faster timelines mean customer churn and expensive R&D investments

structurally_incentivized.name: Quantum computing companies seeking investment
structurally_incentivized.description: Companies like IBM, Google, IonQ, Rigetti, and PsiQuantum have raised billions in capital by promising quantum computing breakthroughs. They are structurally incentivized to emphasize quantum capability timelines that justify investment, while the offensive/defensive implications of those timelines for cryptography are treated as someone else's problem.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Venture capital and public market valuations depend on demonstrating progress toward quantum supremacy; explicitly discussing cryptographic risks would invite regulatory scrutiny and potential restrictions on their technology

structurally_incentivized.name: Intelligence agencies with signals intelligence mandates
structurally_incentivized.description: Agencies like the NSA, GCHQ, and their equivalents in Russia and China have institutional missions centered on signals intelligence. While publicly promoting PQC migration for defense, these agencies may resist universal adoption of unbreakable quantum-safe cryptography, as it would undermine their core intelligence-gathering capabilities.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Institutional mission and budget justification depend on ability to intercept and decrypt communications; truly unbreakable encryption eliminates a core capability

structurally_incentivized.name: Cybersecurity firms profiting from ongoing vulnerability
structurally_incentivized.description: The cybersecurity industry generates revenue from the perpetual cycle of vulnerability and remediation. Companies selling quantum risk assessments, PQC migration consulting, and quantum-safe products benefit from extended uncertainty about Q-Day timelines — too soon and clients panic, too late and there is no market.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Revenue depends on sustained anxiety about quantum threats combined with slow migration; a rapid, standardized, free transition would eliminate the consulting and services market

reform_in_other_systems.name: French ANSSI Post-Quantum Transition Guidance
reform_in_other_systems.description: France's national cybersecurity agency ANSSI published detailed technical guidance in 2022 recommending hybrid approaches — combining classical and post-quantum algorithms — rather than immediate full replacement. This approach acknowledges that PQC algorithms lack the decades of cryptanalysis that classical algorithms have undergone and provides defense-in-depth during transition.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: The hybrid approach has influenced European and international thinking on PQC migration strategy, providing a more conservative but potentially safer path than full replacement. Several other EU nations have adopted similar guidance.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Germany's BSI Post-Quantum Cryptography Recommendations
reform_in_other_systems.description: The German Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) has been among the most aggressive national agencies in pushing PQC readiness, publishing technical guidelines requiring hybrid PQC modes for government systems and recommending the FrodoKEM algorithm alongside NIST selections for additional security margin.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Germany's insistence on algorithm diversity beyond NIST selections provides a hedge against the possibility that any single PQC algorithm family is broken, offering a model for defense-in-depth that the US approach largely lacks.

reform_in_other_systems.name: China's Post-Quantum Cryptography Standards Program
reform_in_other_systems.description: China has pursued an independent PQC standardization track through the Chinese Association for Cryptologic Research (CACR) and the Office of the State Commercial Cryptography Administration, selecting different algorithms than NIST in some categories. China's 2024 announcement of a national PQC standard distinct from US standards signals a potential bifurcation of global cryptographic infrastructure.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: China's parallel standardization creates both risk and resilience: risk because interoperability between Chinese and Western systems may degrade, but resilience because algorithm diversity across geopolitical blocs means a single break would not compromise all global systems simultaneously.

reform_in_other_systems.name: South Korea's Quantum-Safe Network Infrastructure Initiative
reform_in_other_systems.description: South Korea has invested heavily in quantum key distribution (QKD) networks and PQC integration, with SK Telecom deploying commercial QKD services and the Korean government funding a national quantum-safe communication backbone. This combines PQC with quantum-physical layer security for critical infrastructure.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: South Korea's approach of combining QKD with PQC provides defense even if PQC algorithms are eventually broken, but the high cost of QKD infrastructure means this model is only viable for wealthy nations and critical links, highlighting the equity gap in quantum-safe migration.

issues.name: Harvest now, decrypt later attacks already underway
issues.description: Nation-state actors are already intercepting and storing encrypted communications with the intention of decrypting them once quantum capability arrives. Sensitive communications from today are future-compromised.

reformers.name: Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF)
reformers.description: The EFF has been a leading voice in raising public awareness about harvest-now-decrypt-later (HNDL) attacks, advocating for immediate deployment of forward secrecy and post-quantum protections in consumer communications. Their policy work connects mass surveillance concerns — documented since the Snowden revelations — to the quantum threat, arguing that data collected under programs like UPSTREAM is precisely the stockpile that quantum decryption will unlock.

reformers.name: Tanja Lange and Daniel J. Bernstein
reformers.description: Cryptographers who have been among the earliest and most persistent voices warning about the HNDL threat. Bernstein, a professor at the University of Illinois at Chicago and Eindhoven University of Technology, and Lange, a professor at Eindhoven, co-founded the pqcrypto.org initiative and have published extensively on the urgency of deploying post-quantum cryptography before quantum computers arrive — precisely because of retroactive decryption risk.

reformers.name: Cloudflare
reformers.description: Cloudflare has deployed experimental post-quantum key agreement on its global CDN, enabling PQC protection for a significant fraction of global web traffic. By deploying hybrid post-quantum TLS at scale starting in 2022, Cloudflare directly addresses the HNDL threat for data in transit, and its public research and open-source contributions lower the barrier for other infrastructure providers.

reformers.name: Apple
reformers.description: Apple deployed PQ3, a post-quantum cryptographic protocol, in iMessage in March 2024, making it one of the first companies to bring PQC to billions of consumer endpoints. PQ3 uses a hybrid design with periodic PQC rekeying, specifically designed to defeat HNDL attacks by ensuring that even captured ciphertexts cannot be retroactively decrypted. The scale of deployment — over a billion devices — materially reduces the global HNDL attack surface.

reformers.name: Google Chrome Team
reformers.description: Google began experimenting with post-quantum key exchange (NTRU-HRSS, later Kyber) in Chrome as early as 2016, and enabled hybrid ML-KEM key exchange by default in Chrome 124 (2024). As the dominant web browser with roughly 65% global market share, Chrome's adoption of PQC in TLS handshakes protects a large share of web traffic from HNDL attacks and pressures other browsers and servers to follow.

legislation.name: Quantum Computing Cybersecurity Preparedness Act
legislation.description: While primarily focused on future-proofing federal systems, this 2022 law implicitly addresses HNDL by requiring agencies to prioritize migration of systems handling long-duration secrets — precisely the data most valuable in harvest-now-decrypt-later scenarios. The law's inventory requirement forces agencies to identify which data would be most damaging if retroactively decrypted.

legislation.name: FISA Section 702 Reauthorization (2024)
legislation.description: The reauthorization of Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act in April 2024 expanded the legal authority for US intelligence agencies to collect communications in transit — the same collection mechanism that enables HNDL stockpiling. While framed as counterterrorism and foreign intelligence, the expanded authority means more encrypted data is being collected and stored, increasing the volume of material vulnerable to future quantum decryption.

legislation.name: No legal prohibition on state-level HNDL data hoarding
legislation.description: No US law prohibits intelligence agencies or law enforcement from storing encrypted communications indefinitely with the expectation of future decryption capability. Existing data retention frameworks do not account for the possibility that encryption is a time-limited protection, creating a structural legal gap where HNDL is implicitly authorized.

legislation.name: EU Cyber Resilience Act
legislation.description: The EU's Cyber Resilience Act, adopted in 2024, establishes cybersecurity requirements for products with digital elements sold in the EU, including requirements for security updates and vulnerability handling. While not quantum-specific, its requirement that manufacturers provide security updates for the expected product lifetime creates an implicit obligation to address quantum vulnerabilities as they emerge, including HNDL protection.

legislation.name: Executive Order 14028 on Improving the Nation's Cybersecurity
legislation.description: Biden's May 2021 executive order mandated encryption of data at rest and in transit for federal systems, and required adoption of zero-trust architectures. While pre-dating the quantum-specific directives, it established the framework for mandatory cryptographic upgrades and provided the legal foundation for subsequent quantum-preparedness mandates.

structurally_incentivized.name: National Security Agency (NSA) and signals intelligence agencies globally
structurally_incentivized.description: The NSA and its Five Eyes partners, as well as counterparts in Russia (FSB/GRU), China (MSS/PLA), and Israel (Unit 8200), are the primary practitioners of HNDL. These agencies collect and store encrypted communications as a core function, with budgets and institutional mandates built around signals intelligence. The Snowden documents revealed the NSA's UPSTREAM program intercepting data from fiber optic cables — the same infrastructure that enables HNDL at scale.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Institutional mission, multi-billion-dollar budgets, and legal authorities (FISA, EO 12333) are built around intercepting communications; HNDL is a natural extension of existing collection programs and requires minimal additional investment since storage costs decrease annually

structurally_incentivized.name: Data storage companies and cloud providers
structurally_incentivized.description: Companies providing storage infrastructure — from hard drive manufacturers to cloud providers — benefit from the demand generated by massive data hoarding programs. The declining cost of storage makes HNDL increasingly economical; storing a petabyte of data costs a fraction of what it did a decade ago.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Government contracts for data storage at agencies like NSA (the Utah Data Center can store exabytes) represent significant revenue; the more data governments collect and retain, the more storage and infrastructure these companies sell

structurally_incentivized.name: Telecommunications carriers under lawful intercept mandates
structurally_incentivized.description: Under CALEA and similar laws in other countries, telecom carriers are legally required to provide intercept capabilities to law enforcement and intelligence agencies. Companies like AT&T and Verizon have provided infrastructure access for surveillance programs (as documented in the Room 641A revelations). These carriers face legal obligations and financial incentives to facilitate data collection, even when that data is encrypted and being stockpiled for future decryption.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Legal mandates require compliance with intercept orders; carriers are compensated for intercept compliance and face legal penalties for non-compliance; building intercept infrastructure creates ongoing revenue relationships with government agencies

reform_in_other_systems.name: European Quantum Communication Infrastructure (EuroQCI)
reform_in_other_systems.description: The EU has invested in building a quantum communication infrastructure spanning all 27 member states, combining quantum key distribution (QKD) with satellite and fiber links. EuroQCI is designed to protect sensitive government communications with information-theoretic security, making them immune to both current and future computational attacks, directly countering HNDL.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: While still in development and limited to government and critical infrastructure, EuroQCI represents the most ambitious multinational approach to defeating HNDL. Its reliance on physics rather than computational assumptions means it provides protection regardless of when quantum computers arrive. However, cost limits it to highest-value communications.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Singapore's National Quantum-Safe Network (NQSN)
reform_in_other_systems.description: Singapore launched the National Quantum-Safe Network in 2022, a national testbed for quantum-safe communications involving government agencies, financial institutions, and critical infrastructure operators. The NQSN combines QKD with PQC to protect data in transit, with pilot deployments in the financial sector addressing HNDL threats to banking communications.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Singapore's small geographic size makes QKD deployment feasible at national scale, providing a proof of concept for quantum-safe national infrastructure. The financial sector focus addresses one of the highest-value HNDL targets. However, the model is difficult to replicate in geographically large nations.

reform_in_other_systems.name: UK National Cyber Security Centre PQC Migration Guidance
reform_in_other_systems.description: The UK's NCSC issued guidance in November 2023 explicitly calling out HNDL as a present threat and recommending immediate adoption of hybrid PQC for data with long confidentiality requirements. Unlike the US approach, which emphasizes compliance timelines, the NCSC guidance prioritizes data classification — identifying which data is most valuable in an HNDL context and migrating protection for that data first.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: The threat-informed prioritization approach offers a more practical migration strategy than blanket timelines, ensuring the most HNDL-vulnerable data is protected first even if full migration takes years. This risk-based model has influenced guidance in several Commonwealth nations.

issues.name: Catastrophic exposure of historical secrets
issues.description: When quantum decryption arrives, decades of encrypted data become readable — intelligence sources, corporate secrets, medical records, attorney-client communications, journalistic sources — all retroactively exposed.

reformers.name: Freedom of the Press Foundation
reformers.description: FPF, led by executive director Trevor Timm, has worked to strengthen protections for journalistic sources and confidential communications. Their SecureDrop platform for whistleblower communications is being evaluated for post-quantum upgrades. The retroactive exposure of journalistic sources through quantum decryption represents an existential threat to press freedom that FPF has highlighted in policy advocacy.

reformers.name: Bruce Schneier
reformers.description: Cryptographer, author, and security technologist who has been among the most prominent voices warning about the retroactive exposure problem. Schneier has argued that the quantum threat is fundamentally different from other cybersecurity risks because it operates backwards in time — today's encrypted data has a finite shelf life. His writing and Congressional testimony have shaped policy thinking about the unique harms of historical secret exposure.

reformers.name: American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU)
reformers.description: The ACLU's technology and privacy project has advocated for stronger data minimization requirements and encryption standards, including post-quantum protections. Their legal work on surveillance reform directly addresses the retroactive exposure problem: the more data governments collect and retain, the larger the corpus of historical secrets that quantum decryption will expose. ACLU litigation challenging mass data collection programs has implications for reducing HNDL exposure.

reformers.name: Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press
reformers.description: RCFP has advocated for legal frameworks that protect the confidentiality of journalistic sources and communications. The prospect of retroactive quantum decryption threatens every confidential source communication ever encrypted rather than destroyed — a problem RCFP has flagged as requiring both legal protections (privilege) and technical solutions (forward secrecy, PQC migration).

reformers.name: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) Post-Quantum Working Groups
reformers.description: The IETF's PQUIP (Post-Quantum Use In Protocols) working group is developing standards for integrating PQC into internet protocols including TLS, SSH, and IPsec. Their work on forward secrecy mechanisms is particularly relevant to the historical exposure problem — proper forward secrecy ensures that compromise of long-term keys does not retroactively expose past session keys, limiting the damage window of quantum decryption.

legislation.name: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) encryption requirements
legislation.description: HIPAA requires encryption of protected health information (PHI) but does not specify cryptographic algorithm requirements or mandate quantum-resistant encryption. Decades of medical records encrypted with RSA or AES key exchange vulnerable to quantum attack will become readable, exposing genetic information, mental health records, substance abuse treatment, and HIV status for millions of patients. The regulatory framework treats encryption as binary (encrypted/unencrypted) rather than acknowledging that encryption has an expiration date.

legislation.name: Attorney-client privilege lacks technical protection mandates
legislation.description: While attorney-client privilege is a bedrock legal principle, there is no federal requirement specifying the technical strength of encryption used to protect privileged communications. Law firms routinely use standard email encryption that will be quantum-vulnerable. When quantum decryption exposes decades of privileged communications, the legal system has no framework for addressing retroactive privilege violation at scale.

legislation.name: EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Article 32
legislation.description: GDPR Article 32 requires 'appropriate technical measures' for data protection, including encryption. European Data Protection Board guidance is beginning to acknowledge that 'appropriate' must be evaluated against emerging threats including quantum computing. This creates a potential legal basis for requiring PQC migration to protect historical data stores — a framework the US lacks.

legislation.name: Federal Records Act data retention requirements
legislation.description: Federal agencies are required to retain records for specified periods — often decades. These retention mandates mean that encrypted federal records must be stored long enough for quantum computers to potentially decrypt them. The law requires retention but does not require re-encryption with stronger algorithms, creating a structural guarantee that historical federal records will be exposed when quantum decryption arrives.

legislation.name: Proposed Quantum Data Protection Act concepts
legislation.description: Multiple policy researchers and advocacy groups have proposed legislation that would require organizations holding encrypted data beyond a specified time horizon to either re-encrypt with quantum-resistant algorithms or purge the data. No such bill has been formally introduced, representing a critical structural gap in the legal framework for protecting historical secrets.

structurally_incentivized.name: Intelligence agencies holding encrypted foreign communications archives
structurally_incentivized.description: Intelligence agencies worldwide have accumulated decades of encrypted foreign communications they could not read at the time of collection. These archives represent enormous intelligence value that quantum decryption would unlock — decades of diplomatic communications, military planning, and intelligence operations from adversary nations.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Decades of intercepted encrypted communications become the most valuable intelligence windfall in history once quantum decryption arrives; agencies have institutional incentive to maintain and expand these archives rather than support encryption standards that would protect them

structurally_incentivized.name: Litigation financing and legal discovery firms
structurally_incentivized.description: The legal industry's e-discovery sector processes encrypted communications during litigation. Quantum decryption would potentially make decades of encrypted corporate communications discoverable in lawsuits, creating an enormous new market for quantum-enabled discovery tools and services.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Access to previously encrypted corporate communications creates a massive new evidence base for litigation; firms specializing in e-discovery and litigation support would see enormous demand for quantum-decrypted evidence analysis

structurally_incentivized.name: Private intelligence and corporate espionage actors
structurally_incentivized.description: Private intelligence firms, competitive intelligence consultancies, and state-sponsored corporate espionage operations stand to benefit enormously from quantum decryption of historical corporate communications, including trade secrets, M&A discussions, pricing strategies, and proprietary research data encrypted with now-vulnerable algorithms.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Decades of encrypted corporate secrets become accessible, providing competitive intelligence, litigation ammunition, and blackmail material; entities with early quantum access can monetize decrypted corporate data before targets realize they are exposed

reform_in_other_systems.name: Switzerland's data protection framework and encryption culture
reform_in_other_systems.description: Switzerland's strong data protection laws, combined with its tradition of banking secrecy and encrypted communications (ProtonMail, Threema are Swiss companies), have created a culture where organizations proactively invest in the strongest available encryption. Swiss companies are among the earliest private-sector adopters of PQC, motivated by the legal and reputational consequences of historical secret exposure in a jurisdiction that takes privacy obligations seriously.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Switzerland demonstrates that strong legal privacy frameworks create market incentives for proactive cryptographic migration. The contrast with the US, where legal privacy protections are weaker and PQC adoption in the private sector is slower, illustrates how legal frameworks shape technical readiness.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Germany's constitutional right to confidentiality and integrity of IT systems
reform_in_other_systems.description: In 2008, Germany's Federal Constitutional Court established a fundamental right to the confidentiality and integrity of information technology systems (Grundrecht auf Gewährleistung der Vertraulichkeit und Integrität informationstechnischer Systeme). This constitutional right creates a legal foundation for requiring the state to protect citizens' encrypted data against future threats, including quantum decryption.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: This constitutional framework provides a legal basis for citizens to demand that the government invest in PQC migration to protect their historical data. No equivalent constitutional right exists in the US, where courts have not recognized a fundamental right to encryption strength.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Estonia's e-governance and proactive cryptographic migration
reform_in_other_systems.description: Estonia, which runs the most digitized government in the world (e-residency, digital ID, online voting, blockchain-backed registries), recognized early that its entire national infrastructure depends on cryptographic integrity. Estonia's Information System Authority (RIA) began PQC migration planning in 2023, with particular focus on its KSI blockchain infrastructure that underpins all government records.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Estonia's experience shows that countries heavily invested in digital infrastructure have stronger incentives for proactive PQC migration. Their KSI blockchain approach — which uses hash-based signatures already considered quantum-resistant — offers a partial model for quantum-safe record integrity, though it does not address the confidentiality of historically encrypted data.

issues.name: Financial system vulnerability
issues.description: Global financial infrastructure relies on cryptographic protocols that quantum computing threatens. The transition to quantum-resistant cryptography in banking, trading, and payment systems is a massive coordination problem with systemic risk during the migration window.

reformers.name: Bank for International Settlements (BIS) Innovation Hub
reformers.description: The BIS Innovation Hub has conducted research into quantum threats to the financial system, including Project Leap — a collaboration with the Banque de France and Deutsche Bundesbank exploring quantum-safe communications for central banking. BIS serves as a coordination mechanism for central banks worldwide to address systemic quantum risk, which no individual bank can solve alone.

reformers.name: SWIFT (Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication)
reformers.description: SWIFT, which processes messaging for over 11,000 financial institutions across 200+ countries, began post-quantum cryptography testing in 2023. Given that SWIFT messaging underpins trillions of dollars in daily transactions, their migration timeline effectively sets the floor for the global financial system's quantum readiness. SWIFT's Customer Security Programme is integrating PQC requirements.

reformers.name: JPMorgan Chase quantum research team
reformers.description: JPMorgan has built one of the largest quantum computing research teams in the financial sector, with over 60 researchers. While much of their work focuses on quantum applications (optimization, risk modeling), their quantum-safe cryptography initiative has produced published research on PQC integration into financial messaging and has conducted proof-of-concept deployments of quantum key distribution across their network.

reformers.name: Financial Stability Board (FSB)
reformers.description: The FSB, which coordinates financial regulation across G20 nations, has flagged quantum computing as an emerging risk to financial stability in multiple reports. Their work on identifying systemic risks from technology disruptions positions them as the primary international body for coordinating financial sector PQC migration across jurisdictions.

reformers.name: Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS)
reformers.description: MAS has been among the most proactive financial regulators globally on quantum risk, funding quantum-safe network trials for the financial sector through Project Leap and the National Quantum-Safe Network. Their 2023 guidance to financial institutions on quantum risk assessment provides a template for how financial regulators can drive PQC migration without waiting for international consensus.

legislation.name: Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Safeguards Rule — cryptographic gaps
legislation.description: The GLBA Safeguards Rule requires financial institutions to implement encryption for customer data, but specifies no minimum cryptographic strength or algorithm requirements. This means financial institutions can technically comply while using quantum-vulnerable cryptography. The FTC's 2021 update to the Safeguards Rule similarly failed to address quantum threats, leaving a regulatory gap that will persist until the next revision.

legislation.name: PCI DSS v4.0 cryptographic requirements
legislation.description: The Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard v4.0, effective March 2024, requires strong cryptography for cardholder data but references NIST-approved algorithms — which now include PQC standards. While PCI DSS does not mandate PQC, its reference to NIST standards creates a pathway for requiring quantum-safe encryption in future updates. However, the multi-year compliance cycle means meaningful PQC requirements in PCI are likely years away.

legislation.name: EU Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA)
legislation.description: DORA, which took effect in January 2025, requires financial entities in the EU to ensure operational resilience against ICT-related threats, including emerging cryptographic risks. Its requirement for ongoing risk assessment and technology resilience testing creates a regulatory basis for requiring PQC migration in the European financial sector, though specific quantum requirements are not yet mandated.

legislation.name: No US federal mandate for PQC in financial infrastructure
legislation.description: Despite the financial sector being one of the most quantum-vulnerable industries, no US law or regulation specifically requires financial institutions to adopt post-quantum cryptography. The OCC, Fed, and FDIC have not issued quantum-specific guidance. This regulatory gap means migration is left to voluntary industry action, which historically lags behind threats in the financial sector.

legislation.name: Sarbanes-Oxley Act data integrity provisions
legislation.description: SOX Section 302 and 404 require public companies to maintain internal controls over financial reporting, including data integrity. Quantum computing's ability to forge digital signatures theoretically undermines the cryptographic assurance underlying financial data integrity. However, SOX does not address cryptographic resilience specifically, creating a gap where compliance does not equal security against quantum threats.

structurally_incentivized.name: High-frequency trading firms
structurally_incentivized.description: HFT firms like Citadel Securities, Virtu Financial, and Jump Trading benefit from quantum computing's potential to optimize trading algorithms and crack competitors' encrypted strategies. Early quantum adopters in HFT gain asymmetric advantages in millisecond-level market operations where information asymmetry translates directly to profit.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Quantum computing provides algorithmic advantage in portfolio optimization and may enable decryption of competitors' proprietary trading signals; firms with early quantum access gain trading edges worth billions; they have incentive to pursue quantum capability for offensive advantage rather than support ecosystem-wide defensive migration

structurally_incentivized.name: Legacy banking system vendors
structurally_incentivized.description: Companies like FIS, Fiserv, and Jack Henry provide core banking platforms running on decades-old infrastructure (often COBOL-based mainframes) with deeply embedded cryptographic dependencies. These vendors face massive re-engineering costs to make their systems quantum-safe and have financial incentive to delay migration timelines.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Migration costs threaten margins and risk system instability; vendors profit from maintenance contracts on existing systems; the longer the migration timeline, the longer legacy revenue streams continue

structurally_incentivized.name: Cryptocurrency mining operations
structurally_incentivized.description: Bitcoin and other proof-of-work cryptocurrencies use SHA-256 and ECDSA, which have varying quantum vulnerability. Major mining operations with billions invested in classical mining hardware face an existential threat from quantum computing — but also an opportunity if they can be early quantum adopters. This creates a perverse incentive structure where the crypto industry simultaneously fears and races toward quantum capability.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: First entity to achieve quantum advantage in mining could execute a 51% attack or forge transactions; mining operations have incentive to develop quantum capability for offensive use while publicly downplaying the threat to maintain market confidence in existing blockchain security

reform_in_other_systems.name: Banque de France / Deutsche Bundesbank — Project Leap
reform_in_other_systems.description: Project Leap, a collaboration between the Banque de France, Deutsche Bundesbank, and the BIS Innovation Hub, successfully tested quantum-safe communications for central bank operations in 2023. The project demonstrated PQC-protected communication channels for interbank settlement messaging, proving feasibility for the highest-value financial transactions.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Demonstrated that central bank communications can be migrated to PQC without disrupting operations. The project identified key challenges including increased message sizes and latency from PQC algorithms, providing practical data for planning broader financial system migration. This central bank-led approach contrasts with the US model of leaving migration to market forces.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Singapore Financial Sector PQC Trials
reform_in_other_systems.description: The Monetary Authority of Singapore partnered with JPMorgan, SpeQtral, and Toshiba to test quantum-safe communications for financial transactions in 2023, including both QKD and PQC approaches. The trials covered real-world financial messaging scenarios in one of Asia's most important financial centers.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Singapore's regulator-led approach — where MAS actively coordinates and funds industry trials rather than waiting for voluntary adoption — provides a model for proactive financial regulation of quantum risk. Results showed PQC integration was feasible with existing financial messaging infrastructure, addressing a key concern about migration complexity.

reform_in_other_systems.name: UK Financial Conduct Authority quantum risk framework
reform_in_other_systems.description: The FCA has engaged with UK financial institutions on quantum risk through its operational resilience framework, requiring firms to consider quantum threats in their resilience planning. The Bank of England's Prudential Regulation Authority has similarly flagged quantum computing as a forward-looking risk that firms must plan for, integrating it into stress testing scenarios.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: The UK approach of integrating quantum risk into existing regulatory frameworks (operational resilience, stress testing) rather than creating new quantum-specific regulation provides a pragmatic path that other jurisdictions can adopt without waiting for new legislation. This regulatory-nudge model may be faster than the legislative approach.

issues.name: Asymmetric national advantage and geopolitical destabilization
issues.description: Whichever nation achieves quantum supremacy in cryptanalysis first gains an intelligence advantage that dwarfs anything in history. The resulting information asymmetry could trigger preemptive actions, arms races, or miscalculated escalations.

reformers.name: RAND Corporation quantum security research
reformers.description: RAND has published extensive research on the geopolitical implications of quantum computing, including reports on quantum's impact on nuclear deterrence, intelligence asymmetry, and arms control verification. Their 2023 analysis of quantum computing and strategic stability directly addresses the risk that a quantum breakthrough could destabilize nuclear deterrence if one side could decrypt the other's command and control communications.

reformers.name: Center for a New American Security (CNAS) Technology and National Security Program
reformers.description: CNAS has produced policy research on quantum computing's national security implications, including analysis of the US-China quantum race and recommendations for maintaining strategic stability. Their work connects quantum capability development to broader great power competition dynamics, arguing for transparency measures and confidence-building mechanisms to prevent quantum-triggered escalation.

reformers.name: United Nations Institute for Disarmament Research (UNIDIR)
reformers.description: UNIDIR has conducted research on how quantum technologies affect international security and arms control, including the implications for nuclear verification, secure communications between states, and the applicability of existing arms control frameworks to quantum capabilities. Their work represents the primary multilateral effort to address quantum geopolitical risk.

reformers.name: Elsa Kania
reformers.description: Adjunct senior fellow at CNAS and a leading expert on Chinese military technology strategy, Kania has published extensively on China's quantum computing investments and their implications for US-China competition. Her analysis of China's 2017-2030 quantum technology roadmap and the strategic implications of the Micius quantum satellite program has been influential in shaping US policy thinking about asymmetric quantum advantage.

reformers.name: Nuclear Threat Initiative (NTI) — Cyber-Nuclear Security Program
reformers.description: NTI's work on the intersection of cyber threats and nuclear security has expanded to address quantum risks. The prospect that quantum computing could enable decryption of nuclear command-and-control communications, or forge authentication in nuclear systems, represents a catastrophic scenario that NTI has flagged as requiring immediate attention from nuclear powers.

legislation.name: National Quantum Initiative Act
legislation.description: While framed as a competitiveness measure, the 2018 NQI Act implicitly positions quantum computing as a strategic national security asset. The $1.2 billion in authorized funding and establishment of national quantum research centers reflects the view that quantum capability is a geopolitical imperative — accelerating the very race dynamics that create destabilization risk.

legislation.name: CHIPS and Science Act — quantum computing provisions
legislation.description: The CHIPS and Science Act of 2022 authorized billions for quantum information science research as part of broader semiconductor and technology competitiveness legislation. While driving US capability, it frames quantum computing primarily through a competition lens (particularly with China) rather than a stability framework, potentially accelerating arms-race dynamics.

legislation.name: Export controls on quantum computing technology (BIS Entity List)
legislation.description: The Bureau of Industry and Security has placed Chinese quantum computing entities on the Entity List and restricted exports of quantum computing components. While intended to maintain US advantage, export controls also signal adversaries that quantum capability is viewed as a strategic weapon, potentially accelerating adversary investment and reducing opportunities for cooperative approaches to quantum risk.

legislation.name: No international quantum arms control framework
legislation.description: Unlike nuclear technology (NPT), chemical weapons (CWC), and biological weapons (BWC), there is no international treaty or framework governing the development, deployment, or use of quantum computing for cryptanalytic or intelligence purposes. The absence of any arms control structure means the quantum race proceeds without transparency, confidence-building measures, or limitations.

legislation.name: Wassenaar Arrangement — quantum technology additions
legislation.description: The Wassenaar Arrangement on dual-use technology export controls has been updated to include certain quantum technologies, including quantum computers above specific qubit thresholds and quantum sensing equipment. This represents the closest existing framework to international coordination on quantum technology control, though it lacks enforcement mechanisms and major quantum-developing nations (China, notably) are not members.

structurally_incentivized.name: Defense and intelligence establishments pursuing quantum advantage
structurally_incentivized.description: The US Department of Defense (through DARPA, NSA, and service research labs), China's PLA (through its Strategic Support Force and the University of Science and Technology of China), and other national defense establishments are investing heavily in quantum computing primarily for cryptanalytic advantage. These institutions' budgets and missions depend on maintaining or achieving information superiority.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Institutional budgets, career advancement, and national security mandates reward pursuit of quantum advantage over adversaries; cooperative approaches to quantum risk reduction threaten the institutional logic of competitive intelligence agencies

structurally_incentivized.name: Defense contractors with quantum computing programs
structurally_incentivized.description: Companies including Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, Raytheon (RTX), and their international equivalents (BAE Systems, Thales) have quantum computing research programs tied to defense contracts. These firms profit from the competitive dynamic — the more quantum computing is framed as a strategic arms race, the larger the government contracts for quantum development, PQC migration, and quantum-safe defense systems.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Defense contracts for quantum R&D, PQC migration, and quantum-safe systems grow proportionally with perceived threat urgency; the competitive framing of quantum development directly drives procurement spending

structurally_incentivized.name: Nations with aggressive surveillance mandates
structurally_incentivized.description: Countries including China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea that conduct extensive cyber-espionage operations have strong institutional incentives to achieve quantum cryptanalytic capability first. The intelligence windfall from quantum-decrypting adversary communications would provide decisive advantage, creating a first-mover dynamic that accelerates the race and increases instability risk.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: First-mover advantage in quantum cryptanalysis provides intelligence access worth more than any spy network in history; the asymmetric intelligence advantage creates compounding benefits as one side reads the other's strategic communications while its own remain secure

reform_in_other_systems.name: US-China bilateral quantum dialogue proposals
reform_in_other_systems.description: Several think tanks and former officials have proposed US-China bilateral discussions on quantum computing norms, analogous to early nuclear arms control talks. The Carnegie Endowment for International Peace and the Brookings Institution have published frameworks for quantum confidence-building measures, though no formal diplomatic channel has been established.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: No formal dialogue has materialized as of 2025, largely because both nations view quantum computing as too strategically important for transparency. The failure to establish even basic confidence-building measures contrasts with the early nuclear era, when the dangers were so visible that dialogue became inevitable. Quantum risks are less visible, making diplomatic engagement harder to motivate.

reform_in_other_systems.name: EU Quantum Flagship — open science model
reform_in_other_systems.description: The EU Quantum Flagship, a 1 billion euro, 10-year research initiative launched in 2018, operates on a collaborative open-science model with published research and multinational participation. This contrasts sharply with the classified national-security-driven quantum programs in the US and China, demonstrating an alternative model where quantum advancement is pursued transparently.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: The open science approach reduces destabilization risk by making quantum capabilities more predictable and widely distributed, but it also means EU quantum programs advance more slowly than classified national programs. The EU model demonstrates a trade-off between strategic stability and competitive speed that most nations have resolved in favor of secrecy.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Japan's quantum technology international cooperation framework
reform_in_other_systems.description: Japan has pursued a multilateral approach to quantum technology development, establishing the Quantum STrategic industry Alliance for Revolution (Q-STAR) and engaging in quantum research agreements with the US, EU, and Australia. Japan's strategy emphasizes interoperability and shared standards over competitive advantage.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Japan's cooperative approach builds trust and interoperability among allied nations, potentially creating a quantum technology bloc that can share defensive (PQC) capabilities while maintaining collective security. However, it does not address the core destabilization risk from adversary nations pursuing unilateral quantum advantage.

issues.name: Digital identity infrastructure collapse
issues.description: Digital signatures, certificate authorities, blockchain, and identity verification systems all depend on cryptographic assumptions quantum computing invalidates. The infrastructure of digital trust has an expiration date.

reformers.name: Let's Encrypt / Internet Security Research Group (ISRG)
reformers.description: Let's Encrypt issues free TLS certificates for over 300 million websites, making it the world's largest certificate authority. ISRG has begun research into post-quantum certificate formats and transition strategies. Because Let's Encrypt's automated issuance model can push updates to millions of sites simultaneously, it is uniquely positioned to accelerate PQC migration for the web's identity infrastructure — but also represents a massive single point of failure if the transition is bungled.

reformers.name: CA/Browser Forum
reformers.description: The CA/Browser Forum sets standards for certificate authorities and browser trust stores. Their working groups are developing requirements for post-quantum certificates, including hybrid certificates that combine classical and PQC signatures. The Forum's decisions about when to require PQC in the Web PKI will effectively determine the timeline for quantum-safe web identity across the internet.

reformers.name: Vitalik Buterin and Ethereum PQC research
reformers.description: Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin has publicly discussed quantum threats to blockchain and proposed account abstraction and migration mechanisms to transition Ethereum to quantum-safe cryptography. His EIP proposals and public research on lattice-based signature schemes for smart contract wallets address the existential threat quantum computing poses to the $200+ billion Ethereum ecosystem and all assets secured by ECDSA signatures.

reformers.name: World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) — Web Authentication Working Group
reformers.description: The W3C's WebAuthn standard (FIDO2) underpins passwordless authentication using public-key cryptography. The working group is evaluating post-quantum signature algorithms for future versions of the WebAuthn specification, which is critical because authentication credentials created today with quantum-vulnerable algorithms will need to be replaced or re-enrolled system-wide.

reformers.name: David Chaum — xx Network
reformers.description: Cryptographer and digital privacy pioneer David Chaum launched the xx Network, a quantum-resistant blockchain and messaging platform. While the project is early-stage, Chaum's work demonstrates that building quantum-safe digital identity from the ground up is technically feasible, rather than retrofitting quantum safety onto existing infrastructure.

legislation.name: eIDAS 2.0 Regulation (EU)
legislation.description: The EU's revised electronic identification and trust services regulation, adopted in 2024, establishes the European Digital Identity Wallet and updated requirements for qualified electronic signatures and seals. eIDAS 2.0 includes provisions requiring cryptographic algorithms to be updated as threats evolve, creating a legal mechanism for mandating PQC in European digital identity infrastructure — the most forward-looking digital identity regulation globally.

legislation.name: REAL ID Act — no quantum-safe digital component requirements
legislation.description: The REAL ID Act sets standards for US state-issued identification but includes no requirements for quantum-safe digital signatures or authentication. As state IDs increasingly incorporate digital elements (mobile driver's licenses, digital verification), the absence of PQC requirements means the US identity infrastructure is being built on quantum-vulnerable foundations.

legislation.name: ESIGN Act and UETA — digital signature validity framework
legislation.description: The Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (2000) and the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act establish the legal validity of electronic signatures in the US. However, neither specifies minimum cryptographic strength or addresses the scenario where the underlying algorithm becomes insecure. Quantum computing creates a situation where legally valid digital signatures can be forged, but the legal framework has no mechanism for addressing retroactive invalidity.

legislation.name: OMB Memorandum M-23-02 on PQC Migration
legislation.description: OMB's November 2022 memorandum requires federal agencies to submit cryptographic system inventories and migration plans for post-quantum cryptography, including identity and authentication systems. This creates a timeline for PQC migration in federal digital identity infrastructure, though it does not cover state, local, or private sector identity systems.

legislation.name: Blockchain and cryptocurrency — absence of cryptographic upgrade mandates
legislation.description: No regulatory framework requires blockchain networks or cryptocurrency exchanges to implement quantum-safe cryptography. Given that major blockchains (Bitcoin, Ethereum) use ECDSA signatures that quantum computers can break, the absence of requirements means hundreds of billions of dollars in assets are secured by cryptography with an expiration date. Decentralized governance makes mandatory upgrades structurally difficult even if regulations existed.

structurally_incentivized.name: Certificate authorities with legacy infrastructure
structurally_incentivized.description: Commercial CAs like DigiCert, Sectigo, and Entrust have built business models around issuing certificates using RSA and ECC. Migration to PQC certificates requires new infrastructure, new validation processes, and managing the transition of millions of active certificates — a costly disruption to a profitable business model.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Revenue depends on the existing PKI infrastructure; migration costs are borne by CAs while the security benefit accrues to their customers; CAs have financial incentive to delay migration until forced by browsers or regulators

structurally_incentivized.name: Blockchain maximalists and cryptocurrency exchanges
structurally_incentivized.description: Organizations and individuals with large holdings in quantum-vulnerable cryptocurrencies have financial incentive to downplay the quantum threat to maintain market confidence. Exchanges like Coinbase, Binance, and Kraken profit from trading volume that depends on trust in blockchain security. Public acknowledgment of quantum vulnerability could trigger sell-offs and reduce trading revenue.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Market confidence in blockchain security drives trading volume and asset valuations; acknowledging quantum vulnerability would reduce confidence, trading activity, and exchange revenue; exchanges profit more from status quo confidence than from driving expensive quantum-safe migration

structurally_incentivized.name: National digital identity program vendors
structurally_incentivized.description: Companies contracted to build national digital identity systems (Idemia, Thales, Veridos) have typically built systems on classical cryptographic assumptions. Acknowledging quantum vulnerability in recently deployed billion-dollar systems would create liability, reputational damage, and costly retrofit obligations.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Admitting quantum vulnerability in recently deployed or in-development national ID systems would require expensive retrofits, delay revenue recognition, and potentially trigger contractual disputes; vendors have incentive to treat quantum risk as a future upgrade rather than a present design flaw

reform_in_other_systems.name: Estonia's KSI Blockchain for Digital Identity
reform_in_other_systems.description: Estonia's Keyless Signature Infrastructure (KSI), developed by Guardtime, uses hash-based signatures that are already considered quantum-resistant. KSI underpins Estonia's e-governance identity system, providing quantum-safe integrity verification for government records, healthcare data, and digital identities without relying on public-key cryptography.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Estonia demonstrates that quantum-safe digital identity infrastructure is achievable today using hash-based approaches. However, KSI addresses integrity (proving data has not been tampered with) rather than confidentiality or authentication, meaning it solves part of the problem but not all of it. The model is most relevant for record-keeping and notarization functions.

reform_in_other_systems.name: India's Aadhaar — challenges of scale in PQC migration
reform_in_other_systems.description: India's Aadhaar biometric identity system covers 1.3 billion people and relies on PKI for authentication. The scale of Aadhaar makes it a case study in the challenge of PQC migration for national identity systems — any cryptographic change must be rolled out to millions of authentication points across a vast and unevenly connected country.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Aadhaar illustrates the coordination problem: migrating the world's largest digital identity system to PQC is technically daunting, logistically enormous, and politically complex. India's approach to this challenge — not yet publicly articulated — will provide lessons for other large-population nations. The risk of a failed or incomplete migration is proportional to the system's scale.

reform_in_other_systems.name: EU Digital Identity Wallet — PQC-by-design approach
reform_in_other_systems.description: The EU Digital Identity Wallet, mandated by eIDAS 2.0, is being designed with cryptographic agility as a core requirement, enabling seamless algorithm replacement as PQC standards mature. This PQC-by-design approach contrasts with retrofitting quantum safety onto existing identity infrastructure.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Designing for cryptographic agility from the start is significantly cheaper and less disruptive than retrofitting. The EU's approach provides a model for new digital identity systems, though it does not solve the problem for the billions of devices and credentials already deployed worldwide using quantum-vulnerable cryptography.

issues.name: Widening power asymmetry between states and citizens
issues.description: Quantum computing will be available to governments and large corporations long before individuals or civil society, creating a period where the powerful can break the encryption of the less powerful but not vice versa.

reformers.name: Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF)
reformers.description: The EFF has been the most consistent advocate for the principle that strong encryption is a civil rights issue. Their work on the quantum power asymmetry problem extends their decades-long advocacy against backdoors and for universal strong encryption. EFF argues that the period of asymmetric quantum access — where governments can break encryption but citizens cannot — is functionally equivalent to a universal backdoor, and that accelerating PQC deployment for consumer tools is a civil liberties imperative.

reformers.name: Access Now
reformers.description: International digital rights organization that has advocated for encryption rights at the UN level and in national policy debates across 30+ countries. Access Now's work on the intersection of quantum computing and human rights frames asymmetric quantum access as a surveillance enabler, particularly dangerous for activists, journalists, and dissidents in authoritarian regimes who rely on encryption for safety.

reformers.name: Meredith Whittaker
reformers.description: President of the Signal Foundation and former co-lead of Google's Open Research group, Whittaker has been a prominent voice on power asymmetries in technology. Under her leadership, Signal's deployment of PQ3-style post-quantum protections directly addresses the power asymmetry by ensuring that ordinary users have access to quantum-resistant encryption before governments and corporations can deploy quantum computers to break classical encryption.

reformers.name: Tor Project
reformers.description: The Tor Project, which provides anonymity infrastructure used by millions including journalists, activists, and citizens under authoritarian regimes, has begun research into post-quantum protections for the Tor network. Because Tor's anonymity depends on the cryptographic security of its relay system, quantum computing threatens to deanonymize every user — making PQC migration critical for maintaining citizens' ability to resist state surveillance.

reformers.name: Matthew Green
reformers.description: Cryptography professor at Johns Hopkins University who has been a leading public voice explaining the asymmetric power implications of quantum computing. Green's writing and public commentary make the case that PQC migration in consumer tools must be treated as a national security priority — not because of foreign threats, but because the domestic power asymmetry between a quantum-capable government and non-quantum citizens fundamentally alters the relationship between state and individual.

legislation.name: Fourth Amendment — constitutional limits on quantum-enabled searches
legislation.description: The Fourth Amendment protects against unreasonable searches and seizures, but existing jurisprudence has not addressed whether quantum decryption of encrypted data constitutes a search requiring a warrant. The Supreme Court's Carpenter v. United States (2018) decision suggests that digital data deserves strong privacy protection, but no court has ruled on quantum-enabled decryption. The constitutional framework is unprepared for the scenario where the government can simply read all encrypted communications.

legislation.name: EARN IT Act (proposed repeatedly)
legislation.description: The EARN IT Act, introduced in multiple Congressional sessions, would undermine end-to-end encryption by creating liability for platforms that cannot scan encrypted content. In a world where quantum computing creates asymmetric power, laws weakening encryption disproportionately harm citizens who lose their primary protection against quantum-capable state surveillance. The bill has stalled but continues to be reintroduced.

legislation.name: UK Online Safety Act — encryption weakening provisions
legislation.description: The UK's Online Safety Act (2023) includes provisions that could require platforms to scan encrypted content, effectively mandating backdoors or client-side scanning. In the quantum context, weakening encryption for citizens while governments develop quantum capabilities that render remaining protections moot represents a double assault on citizen privacy — legal and technological.

legislation.name: EU Resolution on Encryption (2020 Council Resolution)
legislation.description: The EU Council's 2020 resolution on encryption called for 'lawful access' to encrypted data, which critics interpret as a push for backdoors. Like the EARN IT Act, this creates a dynamic where governments seek to weaken the encryption protecting citizens while simultaneously developing quantum capabilities that will make even strong encryption vulnerable — a compounding asymmetry.

legislation.name: Post-quantum cryptography for consumer products — no requirement exists
legislation.description: While federal agencies are mandated to migrate to PQC under NSM-10 and the Quantum Computing Cybersecurity Preparedness Act, there is no requirement for consumer products, mobile apps, messaging platforms, or IoT devices to adopt quantum-resistant encryption. This structural gap ensures that the government's encryption will be quantum-safe while citizens' encryption remains vulnerable — the definition of power asymmetry.

structurally_incentivized.name: Intelligence and law enforcement agencies opposing universal strong encryption
structurally_incentivized.description: The FBI, DOJ, GCHQ, Australian Federal Police, and other agencies have consistently lobbied against strong universal encryption, arguing for 'lawful access' mechanisms. In the quantum era, these agencies benefit from a window where their quantum capabilities can break citizens' classical encryption — universal PQC deployment closes this window.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: The period of asymmetric quantum access provides intelligence and law enforcement with unprecedented surveillance capabilities without requiring warrants, backdoors, or cooperation from platforms; agencies have institutional incentive to slow PQC adoption in consumer tools while accelerating their own quantum capabilities

structurally_incentivized.name: Surveillance technology vendors
structurally_incentivized.description: Companies like NSO Group (Pegasus), Cellebrite, Palantir, and Clearview AI sell surveillance capabilities to governments worldwide. Quantum computing extends their potential market by enabling decryption of communications that current tools cannot access, creating demand for quantum-enabled surveillance products and services.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Quantum decryption capability can be packaged as a surveillance product sold to government clients; the wider the gap between government quantum capabilities and citizen encryption strength, the larger the market for quantum-enabled surveillance tools

structurally_incentivized.name: Authoritarian governments seeking total information awareness
structurally_incentivized.description: Governments in China, Russia, Saudi Arabia, UAE, and other authoritarian states have invested in surveillance infrastructure and would benefit most from asymmetric quantum access. These governments already have incentive to collect and store encrypted dissident communications for future quantum decryption, and to slow international efforts toward universal PQC adoption.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Asymmetric quantum capability enables retroactive identification of dissidents, journalists, and activists whose encrypted communications were previously unreadable; the power asymmetry converts quantum computing into an authoritarian control tool

reform_in_other_systems.name: Germany's Federal Constitutional Court — encryption as fundamental right
reform_in_other_systems.description: German courts have interpreted the Basic Law's protections of communication privacy and IT system integrity to encompass a right to strong encryption. This legal framework treats the erosion of encryption protection — whether through backdoor mandates or failure to maintain cryptographic strength — as a constitutional violation, providing a legal basis for requiring the state to support PQC deployment for citizens.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Germany's constitutional approach creates a legal obligation for the state to actively protect citizens' encryption, potentially including facilitating PQC migration. This contrasts sharply with the US, where no constitutional right to encryption exists and the government has often sought to weaken it.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Brazil's Marco Civil da Internet — encryption protection
reform_in_other_systems.description: Brazil's Internet Bill of Rights (Marco Civil da Internet, 2014) includes provisions protecting encryption and user privacy. While not quantum-specific, Brazilian courts have used it to defend end-to-end encryption against government attempts to mandate backdoors, establishing a legal framework that could support PQC adoption as a privacy right.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Brazil's experience shows that legal encryption protections can withstand government pressure for access, even in a country with significant surveillance infrastructure. However, legal protections alone do not solve the technical challenge of deploying PQC to all citizens.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Netherlands' encryption policy — government support for strong crypto
reform_in_other_systems.description: The Netherlands has maintained a consistent policy position supporting strong encryption without backdoors, articulated in official government positions in 2016 and reaffirmed subsequently. The Dutch government has explicitly rejected calls for encryption backdoors and has funded research into post-quantum cryptography, positioning citizen encryption strength as a national security asset rather than a law enforcement obstacle.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: The Dutch model demonstrates that governments can frame strong citizen encryption as compatible with national security rather than opposed to it. This framing facilitates rather than hinders PQC migration for citizens, offering a policy model that directly addresses the power asymmetry problem.

issues.name: Pharmaceutical and biotech acceleration without ethical infrastructure
issues.description: Quantum simulation of molecular interactions could dramatically accelerate drug discovery but also accelerate design of novel pathogens, toxins, or psychoactive compounds. The dual-use problem becomes more acute when the design cycle compresses from years to weeks.

reformers.name: Johns Hopkins Center for Health Security
reformers.description: The Center for Health Security has been at the forefront of biosecurity research, including analysis of how emerging technologies (including quantum computing) could accelerate biological threats. Their tabletop exercises and policy research on pandemic preparedness address scenarios where quantum-accelerated molecular simulation enables novel pathogen design, and they advocate for strengthening the Biological Weapons Convention to account for computational biology advances.

reformers.name: Nuclear Threat Initiative — Global Health Security Initiative
reformers.description: NTI's biosecurity program, which developed the Global Health Security Index, has examined how emerging technologies including quantum computing change the biosecurity landscape. Their work on the governance of dual-use life sciences research includes analysis of how quantum-accelerated molecular simulation compresses the timeline for both beneficial drug discovery and potential bioweapons design.

reformers.name: Kevin Esvelt
reformers.description: MIT professor and inventor of CRISPR gene drive technology who has become one of the most prominent voices warning about dual-use biological research risks. Esvelt has argued that the combination of AI and quantum computing will make it possible to design novel pathogens computationally, and has advocated for proactive governance frameworks including 'delay, detect, defend' strategies and restrictions on publishing pandemic-capable pathogen designs.

reformers.name: World Health Organization — Biosafety and Biosecurity
reformers.description: WHO's biosafety and biosecurity program has begun addressing computational biology risks, including the potential for quantum-accelerated molecular simulation to enable novel pathogen design. WHO's Laboratory Biosafety Manual (4th edition, 2020) and its Global Guidance Framework for Responsible Use of the Life Sciences (2022) provide the closest existing international framework for governing dual-use biological research tools.

legislation.name: Biological Weapons Convention (BWC) — gaps in computational biology
legislation.description: The BWC, in force since 1975, prohibits the development and production of biological weapons but was written before computational biology existed. It does not address the use of quantum computers or other computational tools to design pathogens or toxins in silico. The absence of a verification mechanism (the protocol was rejected in 2001) means there is no framework for monitoring quantum-enabled bioweapons research.

legislation.name: Executive Order 14081 — Advancing Biotechnology and Biomanufacturing Innovation
legislation.description: Biden's September 2022 executive order on biotechnology emphasizes the importance of advancing US bio-economy leadership, including using computational tools for drug discovery and biomanufacturing. While it includes biosafety provisions, it primarily frames computational biology as a competitive opportunity rather than a dual-use risk, potentially accelerating quantum-enabled biological research without commensurate safety measures.

legislation.name: Select Agent Regulations (42 CFR Part 73)
legislation.description: US regulations governing possession and use of dangerous biological agents and toxins. The select agent framework was designed for physical agents in laboratories, not for computational design of novel agents. A researcher using a quantum computer to design a novel pathogen in silico would not trigger select agent regulations until the designed agent was physically synthesized, creating a regulatory blind spot during the design phase.

legislation.name: P3CO Framework (Enhanced Potential Pandemic Pathogen oversight)
legislation.description: The HHS P3CO Review Framework, implemented in 2017 after the US gain-of-function research moratorium, governs federally-funded research that could create enhanced potential pandemic pathogens. While applicable to quantum-assisted pathogen design if federally funded, it does not cover privately funded research, foreign research, or computational design that stops short of physical creation. The framework is also widely considered to have weak enforcement.

legislation.name: EU Dual-Use Regulation (2021/821)
legislation.description: The EU's updated dual-use export control regulation includes provisions for emerging technologies that could have military or weapons applications, including computational biology tools. While not quantum-specific, it provides a legal framework for controlling the export of quantum-enabled molecular simulation software and hardware if designated as dual-use.

structurally_incentivized.name: Pharmaceutical companies seeking quantum-accelerated drug discovery
structurally_incentivized.description: Companies like Pfizer, Roche, Merck, and specialized quantum-pharma startups (Menten AI, ProteinQure) are investing in quantum computing for molecular simulation and drug design.
